name: Puerto de las Señales and Alto de La Colladona Loop from Pola de Laviana
name_original: Puerto de las Señales y alto de La Collaona – circular desde Pola de Laviana
description: This difficult 76.2-mile road cycling route from Pola de Laviana climbs Puerto de las Señales and Alto de La Collaona with 8936 feet of gain
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Laviana are set within the green valleys and mountains of Asturias, Spain. The region is characterized by its diverse landscapes, including the Cantabrian Mountains and the Nalón River valley, providing varied terrain for cyclists. Riders can expect a mix of challenging climbs and scenic, rolling countryside, with routes often featuring quiet roads. The area's natural features, such as Peña Mea mountain, contribute to a picturesque backdrop for road cycling. Best road cycling routes around Laviana The most popular road cycling route is Puerto de las Señales and Alto de La Colladona Loop from Pola de Laviana, a 76.2 miles (122.7 km) trail that takes 6 hours 51 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through mountainous terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Roadbike loop from Tolivia, a moderate 28.8 miles (46.4 km) path. This route offers a scenic ride on mostly well-paved surfaces. Local road cyclists also love the Good area for riding on flat terrain – Gargantadá loop from Laviana, a 38.8 miles (62.4 km) trail leading through the Nalón River valley, often completed in about 2 hours 48 minutes. Road cycling around Laviana is defined by green valleys, mountainous terrain, and the Nalón River valley. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to challenging climbs. The routes in Laviana are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from a few reviews. More than 700 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Laviana's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Laviana?
A: Laviana offers a diverse selection of over 20 road cycling routes, catering to various skill levels and preferences. You'll find everything from moderate rides to challenging climbs through the region's scenic landscapes.

Q: What kind of terrain and difficulty levels can I expect for road cycling in Laviana?
A: Laviana's road cycling routes feature a mix of green valleys and mountainous terrain. You can find routes with significant elevation gain, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1067444280/puerto-de-las-senales-and-alto-de-la-colladona-loop-from-pola-de-laviana" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de las Señales and Alto de La Colladona Loop from Pola de Laviana&lt;/a&gt;, which is considered difficult. There are also moderate options, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31857864" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roadbike loop from Tolivia&lt;/a&gt;, and routes with flatter sections, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40374946" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gargantadá loop from Laviana&lt;/a&gt;, which leads through the Nalón River valley.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly road cycling routes in Laviana?
A: Yes, while many routes offer climbs, there are options suitable for less experienced riders or those seeking a more relaxed pace. The climb of La Colladona from Pola de Laviana, for instance, is described as scenic and relatively easy, passing through rolling green countryside. Routes along the Nalón River valley also tend to offer flatter sections.

Q: What are some notable natural features or landmarks I might see while cycling in Laviana?
A: Laviana's routes are set against a backdrop of stunning natural beauty. You can encounter the dramatic Cantabrian Mountains, the picturesque Nalón River valley, and even the distinctive 'Ojo de buey' (Ox's Eye) rock formation on &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5097008" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Mea&lt;/a&gt;. Other highlights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5570790" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuensanta Natural Mineral Spring&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5570810" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pendón River Gorges&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Laviana?
A: The green valleys and mountains of Laviana are beautiful throughout much of the year. Autumn is particularly scenic, especially along the Raigosu river. Generally, spring through early autumn offers pleasant temperatures for cycling, though it's always wise to check the local weather forecast before heading out.

Q: Are there any scenic viewpoints along the road cycling routes in Laviana?
A: Yes, the mountainous terrain of Laviana offers several points with panoramic views. You can find stunning vistas from locations like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5151883" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coto Bello Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1074608" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Urbiés Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, providing excellent spots to pause and appreciate the Asturian landscape.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about their experience in Laviana?
A: The road cycling routes in Laviana are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from several reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse terrain, from challenging climbs to scenic, quiet roads, and the beautiful natural surroundings of the Asturian region.

Q: Are there options for longer, more challenging road cycling routes in Laviana?
A: Absolutely. For experienced cyclists seeking a significant challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1067444280/puerto-de-las-senales-and-alto-de-la-colladona-loop-from-pola-de-laviana" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de las Señales and Alto de La Colladona Loop from Pola de Laviana&lt;/a&gt; offer substantial distances (over 120 km) and considerable elevation gain (over 2,700m), providing a demanding yet rewarding experience.

Q: Can I find loop routes for road cycling in Laviana?
A: Many of the road cycling routes in Laviana are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31857864" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roadbike loop from Tolivia&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40385111" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gargantadá loop from Laviana&lt;/a&gt;, which are popular choices for exploring the region efficiently.

Q: Are there cultural or historical sites near the cycling routes in Laviana?
A: While primarily focused on natural beauty, Laviana also offers glimpses into local culture. The main town of Pola de Laviana provides shops and restaurants. You can also find landmarks like the Puente de la Chalana and Puente de Arco, which are associated with the Nalón River and local festivities.

Q: What kind of surfaces can I expect on the road cycling routes?
A: The routes in Laviana generally feature well-paved surfaces, especially for the designated road cycling trails. Cyclists can enjoy relatively quiet roads, particularly after the initial kilometers, contributing to a peaceful riding experience.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Puerto de las Señales y alto de La Collaona cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as a difficult road ride, demanding a very good fitness level. It involves significant elevation gain over its 123-kilometer length, including ascents of three mountain passes, two of which are first-category climbs.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily consists of asphalt roads, with most surfaces being well-paved and easy to ride. However, be aware that there is a section after Puerto de las Señales descending towards Puebla de Lillo that has a poorer road surface.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter stunning mountain landscapes and several notable features. Highlights include the beautiful horseshoe curves near the summit of Puerto de Tarna, a tunnel cut through rock, and the Tanes Reservoir. At the summit of Puerto de Tarna, you'll also find the 'Fonte la Nalona,' the source of the Nalón river. The route also passes over Puerto de las Señales and Alto de La Colladona (850 m).

faqs.question: Where does the route start, and is there parking available in Pola de Laviana?
faqs.answer: The circular route starts and finishes in Pola de Laviana. As a bustling town, Pola de Laviana offers various public parking options, typically found near the town center or residential areas. Specific parking availability may vary, so it's advisable to check local signage upon arrival.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Given the mountain passes and potential for challenging weather, the best time to cycle this route is generally during the late spring, summer, and early autumn months (May to October). This period offers more stable weather conditions and clearer roads, though mountain weather can change rapidly.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for cycling this route, especially since it's in Redes Natural Park?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling this route. While the trail passes through the beautiful Redes Natural Park, general access for activities like cycling on public roads is usually free. However, always respect local regulations and protected areas.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, so while dogs are generally allowed in public spaces in Spain, bringing a dog along for a 123 km, difficult road ride is not practical or recommended for their safety or yours. If you plan to bring a dog to the area, consider shorter, off-road trails suitable for pets.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other notable trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route shares sections with several other paths. Notably, it overlaps with parts of the Asturian Way (36% of the route), Sendero Histórico: Asturias (18%), [CIMA AS10] Tarna - Las Señales (15%), and [CIMA AS19] San Isidro (Estación invernal) (13%).

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this circular cycling route?
faqs.answer: The estimated time to complete this challenging route is approximately 6 hours and 51 minutes. This duration can vary significantly based on your fitness level, the number of stops you make, and weather conditions.

faqs.question: Are there any specific challenges or strategic tips for tackling this route?
faqs.answer: The ascent to Puerto de Tarna is described as a 'Tour-type' climb, meaning it's long but features generally manageable gradients, requiring sustained effort rather than extremely steep sections. Be prepared for the section after Puerto de las Señales descending towards Puebla de Lillo, which has a poorer road surface, requiring extra caution.

faqs.question: What kind of views can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You can expect stunning mountain landscapes throughout the ride, especially as you ascend the three mountain passes. The route offers panoramic vistas of the Asturian countryside, including the Tanes Reservoir and the rugged terrain of the Redes Natural Park.
